PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 1. Purpose This Overview aims to provide the reader with sufficient information to understand the purpose of NHS Grampian, the key risks to the achievement of its objectives and how it has performed during the year. Should you wish to find out more detailed information we have provided this in the rest of the Performance Report, the Accountability Report and the Financial Statements. NHS Grampian publishes a wide range of information on its services and activities on its website which you can find at: https://www.nhsgrampian.org/ 2. Statement from the Chief Executive Looking back on 2024/25 that despite what has been a very challenging year I remain proud of the way in which we have delivered health care and services to the population of the North East of Scotland. This has been enabled by the continued support of our colleagues, partners and the public during a period of challenge for us all. I am extremely grateful for everything that people in communities across Grampian have done and continue to do to support each other and NHS Grampian and all those providing care services on our behalf. I would like to express my thanks and appreciation to all colleagues for their outstanding contributions and for their relentless professionalism. The care and compassion shown to people in our care and to colleagues has been exemplary, as we have addressed the enduring system pressures and other challenges we have faced. I would also wish to thank all our partners for their collaborative support – our care homes, local authorities, our national health services colleagues and local resilience partnership members. Our whole system co-ordination and collaboration, along with the strength of our local Health and Social Care Partnerships has helped us continue to deliver our vital services to the people of the North East of Scotland. Over the last 12 months we have progressed in delivering our Plan for the Future. A key underlying focus given system pressures and financial constraints in this work has been in relation to ‘Finding Balance’ supporting change across the system considering the three strands of governance (clinical, financial and people including workforce and citizens) whilst maintaining a focus on prevention. System pressures continue to present a challenge and we are further developing and redesigning, with partners, our services to ensure that we can continue to meet the health and social care needs of our population and provide a safe environment for our staff and most vulnerable patients. Many of the changes that we have already made will provide a greater level of care in our communities and build on the excellent foundations established through the integration of health and social care.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW Together with our colleagues, partners and the public we are committed to planning for a better future and transforming our services in support of the delivery of safe, high quality, person-centred and sustainable health services over the next five years and beyond. The financial challenges NHS Grampian faced in 2024/25 are highlighted in the Annual Report and Accounts for this year. This challenging financial position is anticipated to continue into future years and work continues to seek financial savings to support our financial recovery. The Accounts show a small underspend against the revenue budget, but this was only achieved due to additional repayable financial support (known as “brokerage”) being provided to the Board, for a second year, by the Scottish Government. This highlights the severe financial pressures currently being faced by the health system in the North East. In 2024/25 NHS Grampian has also been escalated from Stage 2 to Stage 3 of the Scottish Government’s Support & Intervention Framework because of the Board’s financial position and have benefited from additional support and scrutiny. Escalation to Stage 4 was confirmed on 12 May 2025 due to financial sustainability and the deterioration of the Board’s financial position during 2024/25; and Leadership and Governance following rising concerns about local services and performance against national priorities and standards, including some quality concerns raised by the appropriate regulatory bodies. The Chief Executive Team of the Board have been working to implement a risk-based approach to developing a financial recovery strategy which involves achieving an appropriate balance between our financial position, clinical governance matters, staff wellbeing and prevention. Additional financial recovery measures and strengthening of our financial control framework were approved by the Board and progressed during the year. During the year a significant amount of work has been progressed to deliver the local value and sustainability programme engaging widely with colleagues across the system. The purpose of this programme is to deliver short term efficiency savings. Over the medium to longer term, a route map to strategic change is being resourced and will support transforming services and pathways of care to make them sustainable in terms of quality of care, staffing and finance. I recognise the significant financial and workforce challenges not only facing NHS Grampian but impacting on the whole public sector. We will continue to work with our partners, citizens, and colleagues to explore opportunities for greater collaboration locally, regionally and nationally to ensure provision of high quality, safe, efficient and sustainable services which meet the needs of the population. Our operational performance during the year has been impacted as the system remains under significant pressure due to interconnected challenges, including financial constraints, workforce shortages, and infrastructure and digital limitations. These challenges are affecting both short-term performance and the longer-term trajectory toward NHS Grampian’s 2027 position.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW During this period of reshaping for the future your input and support will be important, and I would thank you in advance for working with colleagues and services as we make the necessary changes over the coming months. It is my intention to retire in 2025 but I look forward for my remaining time in office to working with the Board, Chief Executive Team, our staff, and partners in addressing the challenges that we face, implementing our Plan for the Future and continuing to provide the quality of care that our key stakeholders and the people of Grampian expect from us. Interim Chief Executive

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 3. Background and Principal Activities Grampian Health Board is responsible for leading efforts to improve the health of the people in Grampian, and for providing the health care services that people need. We also provide some specialist clinical and support services to other NHS Boards within the North of Scotland and some national services for the whole of Scotland. 17,000 directly employed staff and a range of independent primary care practices (64 General Medical, 130 Pharmacy, 90 Dental and 53 Opticians) provide the full range of primary, community and specialist health services to the over half-million people who live in Grampian. Services are provided at over 100 locations and where possible in people’s own homes across an area covering 3,000 square miles of city, town and village and rural communities. We are the fourth largest health board in Scotland by general funding allocation. Services are delivered based on a portfolio management structure covering Family services, Surgical and Clinical Support Services, Medical and Unscheduled Care, Mental Health and Wellbeing, Population Health and services across Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray. The arrangements include, a range of Primary and Community Care services delegated to the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) covering Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray. All our direct clinical services are supported by corporate functions such as Infrastructure and Sustainability, Digital, Finance and People and Culture. The IJBs are legally constituted cross-sector bodies providing a focus for NHS Grampian to work in partnership with other agencies, including Local Authorities and Primary Healthcare contractors, in the provision of integrated services to the local communities. Executive Director and Non-Executive members of the NHS Grampian Board are members of the Integration Joint Boards which provide the strategic direction for the integration of adult health and social care. 4. Delivering Healthcare – Finding Balance

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW NHS Grampian’s Delivery Plan which was adopted by the Board in June 2024. NHS Grampian Delivery Plan April 2024 – March 2027. The Delivery Plan covering the period April 2024 to March 2027 has been developed jointly with our three Health and Social Care Partnerships, colleagues, citizens, and wider partners. The plan contained our objectives and key priorities. The agreed objectives over the period April 2024 to March 2027 were:  Strengthen colleague and citizen engagement to improve health (People)  Create the conditions for sustainable change (Places)  Improve preventative and timely access to care (Pathways) Our objectives and priority areas for delivery during 2024–2027 are framed around the key components of our Strategy (‘ Plan for the Future 2022-28 ’), People, Places and Pathways and the 10 Scottish Government Drivers for Recovery. A high level outline of the priorities aligned to the organisation’s strategy and the Scottish Government recovery drivers is illustrated overleaf. NHS Grampian was unable to set a balanced budget for this financial year and working to improve this position over the medium term. This is in part a consequence of the ongoing pressures experienced in the health and care system which is resulting in negative impacts on both those accessing and delivering services. The Board is focused on ‘Finding Balance’ supporting change across the system whilst maintaining a focus on prevention. A number of measures to manage the high pressures experienced to stabilise and transform the health and care services are being progressed whilst balancing the need for fiscal control in both short and longer-term delivery of care. An Integrated Performance Assurance Framework has been developed recognising that performance management is critical to delivering on the shared objectives and priorities in the Plan for the Future.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW Summary of Key Issues Affecting Overall Delivery - Enduring levels of high pressure across the system which impact on the need to re- prioritise capacity and reduce ability to release time to engage with core service delivery has been a significant challenge. - System pressures continue to impact on hospital flow and delivery of Unscheduled Care standards which is leading to increased planned care backlog and waits. Despite much committed and hard work by our staff, deterioration has occurred as evidenced by performance in relation to delayed discharges, 4 hour Emergency Department waits and Treatment Time Guarantee waits, although cancer treatment times have largely been stable. - Ability to balance meeting agreed financial performance whilst delivering planned and urgent/unscheduled care standards together and responding to sustained and unprecedented operational pressures across the system remains an ongoing risk. The ambitious saving targets in place for this financial year have been met. - Workforce, finance, and system pressures in general continue as enduring risks affecting NHS Grampian’s ability to maintain momentum towards delivery of our identified strategic objectives. 5. Going Concern Assessment Grampian Health Board is a statutory body and was established in 1972 under the National Health Services (Scotland) Act 1972 and is responsible for commissioning services for the residents of Grampian. NHS Grampian submitted its 2025/2026 financial plan to the Scottish Government in March 2025. The plan does not represent a break-even position recognising the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. However, despite these financial performance challenges it is assumed the organisation has adequate support from the Scottish Government to continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future. There are no material uncertainties relating to events or conditions to date that, individually or collectively, may cast significant doubt on the ability of the board to continue in its operation. The accounts have been prepared on a going concern basis, as the NHS Board will continue in operational existence and continue to provide services for the foreseeable future, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution.

PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW 6. Summary of Performance Appraisal The Board monitors its performance against a range of quality and performance measures and submits performance information to the Scottish Government and other bodies throughout the year. Accordingly, operational (non-financial) information is taken from live systems and is subject to revision at a future date.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 1. Delivering the Plan for the Future As outlined above, our approach to delivering the ambitions set out in the Plan for the Future is focussed on three pillars (People, Places and Pathways), along with key priorities highlighted by the Scottish Government. Our Annual Delivery Plan is our mechanism to set out the organisation’s priorities for change which the Board (via sub committees), and the Scottish Government seeks assurance on. The Delivery Plan acts as a contract between the NHS Board and the Scottish Government. NHS Grampian’s Integrated Performance Assurance and Reporting Framework (IPARF), approved in 2023/24 and designed to create better alignment against the Plan for the Future and the Delivery Plan continues to inform, improve and develop our performance assurance approach. The Framework describes the: - Principles for performance management and assurance - Values and behaviours underpinning the Framework, particularly the need to approach performance assurance with a spirit of enquiry and learning rather than to be punitive or seek to assign blame for under-performance. - Dual purpose of performance reporting within NHS Grampian; it is not only to provide assurance to the Board and Chief Executive Team whether targets are being met, it also helps internal teams regularly monitor and assess their progress, ensuring actions are effective and aligned with desired outcomes. This data-driven approach enables the identification of areas for improvement and helps justify the organisation's chosen priorities. - The provision of a tiered approach to performance reporting across NHS Grampian.  A continuous improvement approach towards reporting resulting in repeated improvement and revision of reporting by iteration. Annual Delivery Plan 2024/25: Overall Status of Q4 Milestones at 31st March 2025: Of the 100 Deliverables reported in Q4 Milestones set out in the Delivery Plan, by the end of March 2025, 45% are complete at the end of Quarter 4, with 38% subject to minor delay, and 12% significant delays. A further 5% had been postponed.

Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets Our performance against the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance  There were 122,330 A&E attendances in 2024/25, a decrease of 1.8% compared to 2023/24.  ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian remain consistently among the lowest in Scotland.  The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 67.2% (67.8% for March 2024) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 70.6% (67.4% March 2024).  The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2025 was 3,693 of whom 523 waited over 12 hours, compared to 3,478 (370 over 12 hours) in March 2024.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The pressures on Emergency Departments (ED) during the year continue to be well publicised across the NHS in Scotland and the UK. Our local analysis suggests that the ED performance was acting as the bellwether of the pressures experienced right across the whole health and social care system with capacity and flow factors affecting each step in a patient’s pathway combining to manifest at the front door of the hospital affecting the 4-hour performance. Delayed Discharge Reduction in the number of patients awaiting discharge from hospital into a more appropriate care setting, once treatment is complete. Performance  There were 181 patients, of which 160 were more than 3 days delayed at the March 2025 census date (166 at March 2024, of which 154 were delayed more than 3 days)  Over the last year, NHS Grampian’s rate of standard and code 9 delays per 100,000 adult population has been among the lowest five of the 11 mainland Boards; from mid-2020 through to the end of 2023 we generally had the lowest rate.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3. Financial Performance and Position 3.1 Consolidated Accounts The Annual Accounts consolidate the results of Grampian Health Board (NHS Grampian), Grampian Health Board Endowment Funds (Operating as NHS Grampian Charity) (a registered Charity) and the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) (Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire, and Moray). The basis of consolidation, explained in note 1 Accounting Policies on Page 106, is determined by the extent of control NHS Grampian can effectively exercise over each of its partner organisations. All Trustees of the NHS Grampian Charity are members of NHS Grampian. The Board therefore has majority control and, using the principles stipulated by IFRS 10, must consolidate, in full, the financial results of the NHS Grampian Charity into the main Board accounts. Voting membership of each of the IJBs is however split equally between members of NHS Grampian and members of each local council. The Board, therefore, has exactly half of the controlling interest in each IJB and, under IAS 28, is required to consolidate only this share of the net assets or net liabilities, as a Joint Venture, into the main Board accounts. With the whole Grampian Health system continuing to operate under significant operational pressures during 2024/25 there was a continuing need to develop the model of delivery for many of the core services provided by each of the three IJB’s.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS All three IJB’s are continuing to experience underlying financial challenges, which reflect demographic and demand pressures resulting in higher numbers of people receiving support in the community. There continued to be a major focus on preventing admissions, supporting people in the community, and minimising the level of delayed discharges in Grampian hospitals. These efforts were underpinned by developments in digital innovations and the community engagement agenda across all three IJB’s. A key priority continues to be the development of whole system partnership arrangements to embed learning and transformational change into clinical strategy and core service provision moving forward. The IJBs faced operational pressures and due to the worsening financial position over the year, each IJB required an additional transfer of resource from NHS Grampian and their Council partner to achieve balance following depletion of IJB reserves. The level of IJB Reserves at the end of the 2024/25 have been further depleted from those reported in 2023/24. This is due to the requirement to apply Reserves to balance financial performance during 2024/25. PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3.2 NHS Grampian performance against statutory financial targets The results of each of the partner organisations, although consolidated with the main Board accounts for group accounting purposes, do not form part of the statutory financial target set for NHS Boards by the Scottish Government Health and Social Care Directorates (SGHSCD) and are, therefore, not taken into account when considering the Board’s in year financial performance. NHS Boards have the ability to manage small under/overspends (within 1% of resource) of an overall breakeven position across a three- year period (2024-27). The Scottish Government require NHS Boards to meet three key financial targets:  Revenue Resource Limit (RRL) – a resource budget for ongoing activity;  Capital Resource Limit (CRL) – a resource budget for net capital investment; and  Cash Requirement – a financing requirement to fund the cash consequences of ongoing activity and net capital investment. Health Boards are required to contain their net expenditure within these limits, and report on any variation. NHS Grampian was not able to set a balanced revenue budget for the 2024/25 financial year. The financial plan submitted to Scottish Government in March 2024 projected a £59.1 million deficit for the year after forecasting savings of £34.9 million. Scottish Government confirmed additional funding for Health Boards across the year of £17 million, also cost pressures of £22.8 million of which £21.4 million related to additional IJB contributions increased the underlying deficit to £64.9 million. £50.5 million savings were delivered of which £25.2 million are expected to be recurring in nature, these savings delivered beyond our financial plan have been required to offset new cost pressures arising during the year. These cost pressures are predominantly linked to costs associated with non- standard bed spaces, increased demand on our system arising due to demographic change within NHS Grampian, inflationary pressures and drug spend beyond that included in the financial plan. The deficit highlights the level of financial challenge experienced during the year driven mainly by a combination of continuing service pressures and high levels of general inflation. Difficulties in recruitment and high demand for services have resulted in significant cost pressures in a number of areas, with the costs of supplementary staffing being a particular driver. This was compounded by the requirement to provide deficit funding to the three IJBs which largely relates to social care. Formal monthly reporting of the Board’s financial position to the Scottish Government is in place with ongoing dialogue throughout the year relating to funding allocations and other cost control measures that have been progressed during the year to mitigate the Boards financial position. In order to achieve in-year financial balance Scottish Government has provided the Board with financial support in the form of repayable brokerage of £65.2 million for a second year (2023/24 £24.8 million). The brokerage is repayable to the Scottish Government at the point in the future when the Board returns to financial balance. Without this additional support, the Board’s final outturn would have been an overspend of £64.948 million (equivalent to 4.17% of the Revenue Resource Limit). A surplus of £0.253 million against the Core Revenue Resource Limit is reported for 2024/25 reflecting this financial support.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS NHS Grampian has been receiving support from the Scottish Government since November 2023, when the Board was escalated to Stage 2 of the Scottish Government’s Support and Intervention Framework. Further escalation took place in January 2025, when NHS Grampian was escalated to Stage 3 of the framework based on the deterioration in the financial position of the Board during 2024/25 and the recommendation of the National Planning and Performance Oversight Group. Senior officers from within NHS Grampian continue to work closely with the Scottish Government to identify and implement solutions to return the Board to financial balance. In 2024/25 NHS Grampian has also been escalated from Stage 2 to Stage 3 of the Scottish Government’s Support & Intervention Framework because of the Board’s financial position and have benefited from additional support and scrutiny. Escalation to Stage 4 was confirmed on 12 May 2025 due to financial sustainability and the deterioration of the Board’s financial position during 2024/25; and Leadership and Governance, it will bring enhanced scrutiny and support from the Scottish Government to develop and deliver a Recovery Plan. NHS Grampian submitted its’ 2025/26 Financial Plan to the Scottish Government in March 2025. The plan does not present a break-even position, recognising the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. Scottish Government has instructed NHS Grampian to reduce the scale of the deficit in both 2025/26 and the remainder of this plan and a Recovery Plan will reflect this. The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2025/26 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium-Term Financial Plan. It recognises the significant work to be undertaken to bring the Board back into sustainable financial balance. The plan takes a risk-based approach recognising the need to identify and contain operational overspends without compromising on the level and quality of services NHS Grampian provides. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover (known as “brokerage”) to support the projected revenue deficit. It is expected NHS Grampian will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution without transfer of services or functions to another entity with associated funding arrangements. 3.2.1 Delivering Sustainable Healthcare – Finding Balance This was the second successive year that NHS Grampian has overspent against its revenue budget and highlights the severe pressures that the health system faces in the North East of Scotland. Accordingly, the Health Board is required to reflect the contribution to IJB funding for devolved health services, and the subsequent commissioning income from the IJB for those services delivered by the Health Board, as a distinct and separate transaction from the operational expenditure incurred delivering those services. The consequence of this in the Health Board’s accounts, is income of £733.397 million (2023/24 £700.871 million), expenditure of £716.290 million (2023/24 £683.102 million).

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3.2.5 Property Valuation All property was revalued by the Valuation Office Agency (VOA), independent specialists in property valuation, on the basis of market value, depreciated replacement cost or existing use value as at 31 March 2025. The values were calculated in accordance with the Royal Institute of Chartered Surveyors Statement of Asset Valuation Practice and Guidance notes, subject to the special accounting practices of the NHS and applied to reflect local market conditions at 31 March 2025. The effect of this revaluation on individual assets was some gained value while others were impaired. The impact of those assets subject to an upward movement in valuation was an increase in the revaluation reserve of £12.964 million and assets subject to a downwards valuation seeing a reduction in the revaluation reserve of £3.241 million. Write down of previous increases of £5.748 million also undertaken. Thus making the overall net impact on the revaluation reserve in relation to the revaluation completed by the VOA of an increase of £9.723 million (2023/24 £44.791 million increase). The overall increase in property value reflects an increase in construction industry prices during the year, impacted by rising demand and a range of economic and geopolitical factors causing supply bottlenecks, labour shortages and increased materials costs. IAS 36 requires that impairments charged to the Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure (SOCNE) in prior years should be reversed to offset any subsequent increase in value relating to that particular asset. The net value of impairment reversal on those assets subject to an upward movement in valuation, was £5.951 million (2023/24 £0.728 million upward impairment) and this was credited to net operating expenditure within the Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure. Further information is available in the section on key sources of judgement, estimation, and uncertainty in note 1 accounting policies, on Page 106. 3.2.6 Pension Liabilities The accounting policy for pensions is provided in Note 1 to the Accounts and disclosure of the costs is shown within Note 19 and the Remuneration Report. 3.2.7 Infrastructure and Non-Current Assets The Board delivered a programme of infrastructure investment totalling £88.9 million during 2024/25. Fraud, Bribery and Corruption NHS Grampian has a zero tolerance for fraud, bribery or corruption and has robust procedures in place, to reduce the likelihood of fraud occurring. These include an agreed policy on the Prevention, Detection and Investigation of Suspected Fraud, Theft and Corruption, Standing Financial Instructions incorporating Standards of Business Conduct and arrangements for the disclosure of information in the corporate register of interests, gifts, and hospitality. Staff are briefed regularly on counter fraud matters including on the confidential routes that are available to report suspected fraud, bribery, or corruption. An awareness program was undertaken in the year focusing on promoting awareness on a number of areas as part of talent development for aspiring managers along with targeted development sessions to raise awareness on health sector fraud and general awareness for reporting concerns. NHS Grampian works closely with other organisations, including NHS Scotland Counter Fraud Services (CFS) and other government agencies to combat fraud. This includes participation in the bi-annual National Fraud Initiative which is a data matching exercise. The Board also takes part in a post payment patient exemption eligibility verification system, which covers all Family Health Service expenditure. 5. Payment Policy NHS Grampian is committed to supporting the Scottish Government in helping businesses during the current economic situation by paying bills more quickly. The intention is to achieve payment of all undisputed invoices within 10 working days, across all public bodies and NHS Grampian is actively working towards achievement of this accelerated payment target.  In 2024/25, the average credit taken was 12 days (2023/24 14 days).  In 2024/25 95% of invoices by value (2023/24 93%) and 94% of invoices by volume (2023/24 90%) were paid within 30 days.  In 2024/25 90% of invoices by value (2023/24 87%) and 88% of invoices by volume (2023/24 79%) were paid within 10 days. The small improvement in payment performance compared to the prior year reflects work to support the purchase to pay process during the year despite challenges around the impact of wider system pressures on support teams. 6. Additional information can be found in the NHS Grampian Annual Climate Emergency and Sustainability Report 2023/2024 prepared in compliance with Climate Change (Duties of Public Bodies: Reporting Requirements) (Scotland) Order 2015 as amended by the Climate Change (Duties of Public Bodies: Reporting Requirements) (Scotland) Amendment Order 2020 Governance The NHS Grampian acknowledges that an effective response to climate change and the planning and delivery of the required sustainable development initiatives require active and meaningful engagement, supported by a robust governance and management framework. The Board recognises its obligation being:  The Climate Change (Scotland) Act 2009 originally set out measures adopted by the Scottish Government to reduce emissions in Scotland by at least 80% by 2050. The Climate Change (Emissions Reductions Targets) (Scotland) Act 2019 amended this longer-term target to net-zero by 2045, five years in advance of the rest of the UK.  In 2020, The Climate Change (Scotland) Amendment order came into force to reflect this and now requires NHS Boards to report on their progress in delivering their emissions reduction targets.  NHS Scotland as a public body must reduce the greenhouse gas emissions from its activities, the activities under its control and from the electricity, steam and heat purchased by it to net-zero by 2040 or earlier where possible. All NHS owned buildings must be heated from renewable sources by 2038 or earlier where possible.  NHS Grampian is required to submit an annual Public Bodies Climate Change Duties report to the Sustainable Scotland Network detailing compliance with the climate change duties imposed by the Act and the Amendment order. The information returned by the Board is compiled into a national analysis report, published annually.

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Sustainability and the Environment (Cont’d) Strategy Development NHS Grampian’s Climate Emergency and Sustainability Strategy Reimagining the Health Service for People & Planet for 2023-2028 serves as the cornerstone of our efforts, and emphasises the necessity of a holistic approach to mitigate and adapt to the impacts of climate change. This strategy is designed to ensure a sustainable health system where planetary and human health are fundamentally intertwined. Our commitment entails embedding sustainability principles across all healthcare activities, supporting communities, and collaborating with stakeholders. These principles are foundational to Grampian’s Plan for the Future and reflect our stewardship aspirations within our healthcare system as an Anchor Institution in the North East of Scotland. Additionally, we have a Heat & Power Strategy which outlines our approach to progressing towards net zero, and a Biodiversity Strategy which details our plans to enhance the natural environment across our estate. The Framework of our sustainability strategy focuses on four main themes:  Delivery of Net-Zero  Greening Health Systems;  Greening Places & Communities; and  Collaboration across local and national systems. The Board is committed to reducing carbon emissions across our estate for both current and new developments. The Board continues to implement a range of initiatives under the wider sustainability agenda, including transitioning to hydrogenated vegetable oil (HVO) for our back-up generators, feasibility studies for deep geothermal site decarbonisation, reducing clinical waste production, embedding alternative transport for our staff (eBikes) which reduces our emissions and improves active travel and wellbeing. There is a considerable challenge in the pursuit of reducing emissions and achieving net zero status. The significant high cost associated with transitioning to renewable energy sources and implementing sustainable practices across various sectors remains an obstacle, especially during a period marked by financial constraints within the organisation. Work is ongoing to develop a route map to achieve carbon zero including an energy and utility strategy for the Foresterhill site, including working closely with partners to explore regional solutions and alternative funding mechanisms. It is anticipated that significant investment will be required for new energy infrastructure and retrofit of existing building infrastructure as part of net zero delivery requirement. NHS Grampian is developing and implementing the actions outlined in our Climate Emergency & Sustainability Strategy. These actions, which encompass climate change adaptation, environmental sustainability and building on our role as an anchor organisation

PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Sustainability and the Environment (Cont’d) actions will be assigned and confirmed within 2025-26, ensuring they are integrated into the organisation's core operational practices and strategic decision-making processes. Communication, Involvement, Engagement and Consultation Addressing these challenges demands a collaborative effort with partners and stakeholders, alongside targeted financial support. Additionally, establishing policy frameworks that prioritise feasibility and affordability is essential in the pursuit of a sustainable, net zero future. The promotion of sustainability remains a key priority for the organisation. This is as much about hearts and minds and helping everyone take individual action, however small. Our robust network of Sustainability Champions are actively enhancing sustainability practices within their departments and cultivating grassroots initiatives across the organisation. This is promoted a culture of sustainability stewardship within NHS Grampian. We have launched a sustainability eLearning module, along with highlighting success stories and practices within the organisation throughout the year. In addition, we will maintain our efforts to host awareness sessions with the Board, focusing on highlighting both successes and challenges in our sustainability initiatives Risk Management As part NHS Grampian identifying and assessing climate change risks it completed a Climate Change Risk Assessment (CCRA) in 2023 to identify impacted healthcare assets and climate hazards. Based on this assessment, an adaptation plan has been developed with measures to address the identified risks. There is ongoing workshops pertaining directly to flood related risks and the tools available for the Board to access and assess the dynamic nature of flooding risks across the estate, which informs related decisions made within the property, maintenance and project teams. There is ongoing work taking place in which Datix is being utilised for the identification of climate change hazards and their direct impact through the creation of new climate change categories. The Board is working to a point where climate change risks such as heat and flooding events can be differentiated from that of user and infrastructure related impacts of the same type. Climate related risks currently sit as part of the infrastructure corporate risk rather than an individual risk at the moment. There is a plan in place for the climate related risks to be managed on a case by case situation whereby they will have an identified risk owner and handler, and will sit within the Civil Contingencies team. There will be a reporting route through NHS Grampian’s Compliance Group and governance through the Sustainability Governance Group.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT i) THE DIRECTORS’ REPORT CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT THE DIRECTORS’ REPORT 1. Naming Convention NHS Grampian is the common name of Grampian Health Board. NHS Grampian Charity is the common name for the Grampian Health Board Endowment Funds. 2. Date of Issue The Accountable Officer authorised these financial statements for issue on 27 June 2025. 3. Going Concern After making enquiries, the Board has a reasonable expectation that the organisation has adequate resources to continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future. The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2025/26 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium Term Financial Plan. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover to support the projected revenue deficit. It is expected NHS Board will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future and continue to provide services, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution without transfer of services or functions to another entity with associated funding arrangements. Accordingly the Board continues to adopt the going concern basis in preparing the financial statements. Further details regarding the adoption of the going concern basis are included in Note 1 of the accounts on Page 106. 4. Accounting Convention In accordance with IFRS 10 – Consolidated Financial Statements, the Annual Accounts consolidate the results of the NHS Grampian Charity Funds (operating as NHS Grampian Charity). Any intra-group transactions between the Board and the Charity Funds have been eliminated on consolidation. The Annual Accounts also consolidate the Board’s interest in the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs); Moray, Aberdeen City and Aberdeenshire, established under the terms of the Public Bodies (Joint Working) (Scotland) Act 2014. In accordance with IFRS 11 – Joint Arrangements, each IJB is considered to be a Joint Venture and under IAS 28 – Investments in Associates and Joint arrangements, the basis of consolidation used is the equity method of accounting. The Annual Accounts and Notes have been prepared under the historical cost convention

Appointment of Auditor The Public Finance and Accountability (Scotland) Act 2000, places personal responsibility on the Auditor General for Scotland to decide who is to undertake the audit of each health body in Scotland. For the financial years 2022/23 to 2026/27, the Auditor General appointed Grant Thornton UK LLP, to undertake the audit of Grampian Health Board. The general duties of the auditor of health bodies, including their statutory duties, are set out in the Code of Audit Practice issued by Audit Scotland and approved by the Auditor General which can be accessed on the Audit Scotland website at the following link: https://www.audit-scotland.gov.uk The Trustees of NHS Grampian Charity appointed CT Audit Limited as external auditor, for the financial year 2024/25. 6. Role of the Board Grampian Health Board was established in 1972 under the National Health Services (Scotland) Act 1972 and is responsible for commissioning services for the residents of Grampian, a total population of over half a million people. NHS Boards working in partnership with Integration Joint Boards, form a local health system, with governing boards responsible for improving the health of their local populations and delivering the healthcare they require. The role of the Board is to: a) deliver patient centred, safe and effective health care to the population of Grampian; b) improve and protect the health of local people; c) improve health services for local people; d) reduce health inequalities; e) focus clearly on health outcomes and people’s experience of their local NHS system; f) promote integrated health and community planning by working closely with other local organisations; and g) provide a single focus of accountability for the performance of the local NHS system.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT All Board members are also trustees of the NHS Grampian Charity, a registered charity established by the NHS (Scotland) Act 1978 and subject to the legal framework of the Charities and Trustee Investment (Scotland) Act 2005. The charitable purpose of NHS Grampian Charity is to enhance healthcare and patient welfare in Grampian, through: a) improvement of the physical and mental health of the Grampian Health Board’s population and our staff; b) prevention, diagnosis and treatment of illness; c) provision of services and facilities in connection with above; and d) research into any matters relating to the causation, prevention, diagnosis or treatment of illness, or any other matters relating to the health service as the trustees see fit. Under the terms of the Public Bodies (Joint Working) (Scotland) Act 2014, three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs), Moray, Aberdeen City and Aberdeenshire, are established in Grampian with full delegation of functions and resources to enable integration of primary and community health and social care services. Executive and Non-Executive members of Grampian Health Board are appointed to represent the Board as voting members on each IJB. The voting membership, and therefore the exercise of control over each IJB, is shared equally between Grampian Health Board and the relevant Local Authority. 7. Board Membership Under the terms of the Scottish Health Plan, the Health Board is a board of governance whose membership will be conditioned by the functions of the Board. Members of Health Boards are selected on the basis of their position or the particular expertise which enables them to contribute to the decision making process at a strategic level. The Health Board has collective responsibility for the performance of the local NHS system as a whole, and reflects the partnership approach, which is essential to improving health and health care. The Plan was produced following more than a year of extensive engagement with citizens, partners and colleagues and set out the strategic priorities required to deliver long term sustainability of the health and care system in Grampian. Central to this ambition of long term sustainability is the need to support the population to live more years in good health. The Plan for the Future acknowledges that this requires NHS Grampian to work with the public and partners to support the step change required to enable wellness and prevent ill health, whilst ensuring timely access to diagnostics and responding to illness. A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT In support of this, a Three Year delivery plan 2024-2027 was agreed by the Board as part of a single coherent plan including the Annual Delivery Plan and Medium Term Financial Plan for NHS Grampian. This Delivery plan sets out how NHS Grampian will make progress against the priority areas and strategic intent within the Plan for the Future for 2024 – 2027 in response to the 10 recovery priority areas of the Scottish Government. The Delivery Plan 2024 – 2027 was formally adopted by the Board in June 2024, approved by Scottish Government by letter dated 28 May 2024. The Plan for the Future strategic intent will be delivered through a rolling programme of priority projects and actions, with defined milestones, articulated in a five year rolling Delivery Plan. The Medium Term Financial Framework details the financial position for the next 5 years, reflects our route map to recovering the deficit position. As a result of the continued significant operational pressures being experienced across the health and social care system, plus the high level of inflation, it will not be possible to return to financial balance without significant redesign of the whole health and social care system. In addition the plan recognised that this will not be without challenge or difficult decision making and ensure that healthcare demands are effectively managed without compromising the quality or safety of care delivered. Performance reporting is a critical mechanism through which the Board maintains oversight of delivery against the Plan for the Future and the Annual Delivery Plan (ADP) 2024–2027. The introduction of the Integrated Performance Assurance and Reporting Framework (IPARF) has strengthened NHS Grampian’s internal governance by embedding a coherent, system-wide approach to both performance management and performance assurance. It supports the organisation to evaluate progress, surface risks, and take action to support delivery against strategic priorities and national expectations. These arrangements are underpinned by a multi-layered assurance model which promotes transparency and accountability across the system. Quarterly performance reporting of the ADP includes the CET ADP Performance Report, which supports internal performance review and scrutiny by the Chief Executive Team; the PAFIC Performance Report, which provides formal assurance to the Performance Assurance, Finance and Infrastructure Committee (PAFIC) on behalf of the Board; and the How Are We Doing (HAWD) Board Performance Report, which offers a summarised, public-facing view of performance. This structured approach enables the Board to scrutinise performance meaningfully and provide clear, evidence-based assurance to the Scottish Government, elected representatives, and the public. 4.3 Management arrangements 4.3.1 Portfolio and system leadership model NHS Grampian continued with the portfolio and system leadership approach endorsed by the Board in August 2021. A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 4.3.2 The Grampian Operational Pressure Escalation System (G-OPES) The G-OPES model to escalate responses to demand pressures in the system continued to be used throughout 2024/25. G-OPES provides: A single approach to evaluate pressure across the health and care system A system view focusing on pathways of care supported by Portfolio leadership  Options to increase capacity through risk/pressure-based derogations of some  standards A whole system approach to share risk and offer internal mutual aid  Clarity for staff  Transparency  Specific protection of time-critical planned care  G-OPES includes at its highest levels a series of derogations to normal practice, which require decisions balancing three dimensions of quality of care against each other and using a risk and ethical based approach to reach a better overall position. Decisions about the derogations applied using G-OPES are taken by the system leadership. The pathways in G-OPES were reviewed in the year to ensure that they remain fit and aligned with Business Continuity and Service recovery plans to ensure responses to critical incidents or major incidents are managed through simple and user friendly command and control arrangements Regular daily or weekly meetings as appropriate confirm the G-OPES levels and the measures being applied will be recorded including the rationale for the decisions as outlined in the paper on the agenda. This includes escalate of more complex situations, or decisions where the risk lies outside the agreed tolerances to the Chief Executive Team. 5. Purpose of the System of Internal Control The system of internal control is based on an ongoing process designed to identify, prioritise and manage the principal risks facing the organisation. The system aims to evaluate the nature and extent of risks, and manage risks efficiently, effectively and economically. The system of internal control is designed to manage rather than eliminate the risk of failure to achieve the organisation’s aims and objectives. As such, it can only provide reasonable and not absolute assurance. The process within the organisation accords with guidance from Scottish Ministers in the SPFM and supplementary NHS guidance, and has been in place for the year up to the date of approval of the annual report and accounts. 6. A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT Ministers. The general principles for a successful risk management strategy are set out in the SPFM. Risk Management is a key element of the NHS Grampian Board’s internal controls and approach to governance. NHS Grampian’s Risk Management Policy outlines that all identified risks which have the potential to cause harm to patients, staff, contractors, the environment, our strategies, plans and objectives, should be managed in line with the NHSG Risk Management Protocol. Risks are proactively managed either at an operational, portfolio or executive level, with appropriate channels of escalation in place via governance structures. To support the success and effectiveness of NHS Grampian’s service delivery and governance arrangements, an enterprise risk management approach is adopted, which seeks to uniformly identify, assess and manage risk in a proactive manner. This approach is formalised within the NHS Grampian Risk Management Protocol. Enduring risks, which are risks that are ever-present within the system, are managed using controls and mitigations so that they present as low a threat as reasonably practical. The Risk Management Information System, Datix, is utilised to document and manage risk, within Risk Registers. All risks are rated using the NHS Scotland Risk Matrices. NHS Grampian’s Strategic Risk Register aims to identify and articulate significant risk that has the potential to impact upon the achievement of the organisation’s strategic objectives, as outlined in Plan for the Future. The effective identification and management of the Board’s Strategic Risks aids assurance that activities and the prioritisation of resources are aligned with the achievement of operational priorities and strategic objectives. During the last year, activities to further strengthen the Board’s approach to managing Strategic Risk were carried out, including enhanced oversight and reporting on the Board’s intolerable risk profile. Within this process, the Chief Executive Team are accountable for the development and management of Strategic Risk, performing regular review activities at their Performance and Business meetings. Board Committees scrutinise deep dive reports presented for their aligned risks, assessing the efficacy of control measures and mitigations, and the level of assurance that can be provided for the management of the risks. The Audit and Risk Committee have responsibility for the scrutiny of the Strategic Risk Register in its entirety, and for oversight for monitoring the effectiveness of arrangements for the NHS Grampian Board’s systems and processes for risk management. The Audit and Risk Committee have responsibility overseeing and a key focus during 2024/2025 has been improving the formal recording of Action Planning relating to risks documented within the organisation’s risk registers. 6.1 Risk Appetite

To support the effective assessment and management of risk within NHS Grampian, a Risk Appetite Statement is used to communicate the amount and type of risk that the organisation aims to seek, accept or tolerate in pursuit of achieving its strategic objectives. Subsequent to a Board Seminar on 9th January 2025, NHS Grampian’s Risk Appetite Statement was updated and accepted by the Board on 10th April 2025. The Risk Appetite Statement is reviewed on an annual basis, or if there are any significant changes to the operating environment. 6.2 Escalation and Assurance for Strategic Risk NHS Grampian’s Strategic Risk Register aims to identify and articulate significant risk that has the potential to impact upon the achievement of the organisation’s strategic objectives, as outlined in Plan for the Future. The effective identification and management of the Board’s Strategic Risks aids assurance that activities and the prioritisation of resources are aligned with the achievement of operational priorities and strategic objectives. During 2024/2025 the new process of oversight, accountability and management of Strategic Risk commenced, aimed at improving the control environment within this area. The Chief Executive Team have accountability for the development and management of Strategic Risk within NHS Grampian. To assist in the management of these risks, regular formal review activities are undertaken during CET Performance or Business Meetings. During reviews, individual risks are considered against the entire Strategic Risk profile and against the Board’s Risk Appetite. The format and cycle of reporting and review supports the robust assessment of Strategic Risk and efficacy of control measures and mitigations, from a system wide perspective. Any gaps in controls or assurances during review are highlighted and actions identified. Strategic Risks are aligned to Board Committees in accordance with their remit and Terms of Reference. The Board Committees conduct an overseeing role, which encompasses scrutiny regarding the management of their aligned risk(s), including associated controls, mitigations, performance and related issues. The Audit and Risk Committee have delegated responsibility, on behalf of the Board, for oversight and scrutiny of the Strategic Risk Register in its entirety, and associated management activities. The Committee is provided with the Strategic Risk Register at each meeting and any assurance notes regarding individual Strategic Risk scrutiny at Other Board Committee meetings. The Committee also include exception reporting to the Board on any serious risk management issues. The Strategic Risk Register is routinely reported to the Board throughout the year for awareness and oversight, the last report being presented in December 2024.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 6.3 Escalation and Assurance for Operational Risk All Operational Risks sit within portfolios, which are aligned to members of the Chief Executive Team. The executive lead for that portfolio of services is accountable for maintaining risk in line with the Risk Management Protocol. In addition to this hierarchy of management control all new operational risks are considered at a system wide Clinical Risk Management (CRM) meeting held on a weekly basis hosted within the Medical and Nurse Directorate. The CRM group consider each risk and make a decision to request further information, and to escalate to the weekly Chief Executive Team meeting, to monitor within CRM or to take no action and pass back for service management. A focus over the last year has been the identification of Risk Leads within areas/portfolios, and an improvement in compliance with risk review and risk action plan requirements across operational risk registers. 7. Internal Control Matters The organisation is committed to a process of continuous development and improvement, developing systems to address the key risks faced by the Board and in response to any relevant reviews and developments in best practice. The Audit and Risk Committee closely monitor progress against all high-risk actions arising from internal audit reports. There were 9 Internal Audit reviews completed during 2024/25. Three of these reviews were rated as high risk (Cyber Security follow up, Backlog Maintenance, linked to the strategic high risk and Business Continuity arrangements) and 2 rated as medium risk (Risk Management and Value and Sustainability framework) these highlighted some weaknesses known to management that are reflected in their findings. Internal Audit in preparing their opinion for 2024/25 have concluded that these findings have allowed them to confirm: governance, risk management and control in relation to business critical areas is generally satisfactory. However, there are some areas of weakness or non-compliance in the framework of governance, risk management and control which potentially put the achievement of objectives at risk. Some improvements are required in those areas to enhance the adequacy and effectiveness of the framework of governance, risk management and control. A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 7.1.1 Heightened Cyber Security and Fraud risks The Board have been cognisant of the heightened risk of cyber security and fraud and the increased number of scams targeted at both individual members of the public and organisations such as NHS Grampian, providing essential services. The following arrangements have been in place throughout the year:  Additional multi-tiered cyber security tools to provide better levels of protection at both the network border and endpoints.  Daily cyber threat hunting activity by the Cyber security team to check and act on vulnerabilities.  Additional leadership and assurance processes to support improved resilience and improve delivery of digital security  Regular reminders were issued using the daily briefing asking all staff to remain vigilant to the heightened risks.  Increased communication around first line of protection, passwords, was undertaken that has seen a reduction in non-compliant passwords from c24% to >1% within the year  A Cybersecurity Week campaign took place to promote awareness and provide best practice and pertinent guidance to staff and relevant to staying cyber-safe at work and at home.  A detailed refresh of the Boards Standing Financial Instructions and Schedule of Reserved Decisions were formally adopted by the Board in April 2024 and were supported by an awareness raising campaign.  The Audit and Risk Committee hosted a development session in September 2024 which focused Counter Fraud and Audit arrangements.  The Audit and Risk Committee also received regular briefings on counter-fraud matters, including awareness raising efforts, progress with ongoing investigations, work in implementing the Counter Fraud Standard and progress and the results of the National Fraud Initiative. The Committee also received regular progress reports re: Internal Audit findings in relation to cyber-security. Arrangements being progressed during the year with delivery in progress include:  Additional enhancements to our cybersecurity control environment in response to identified and emerging risks.

A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT  Governance arrangements for our major capital projects now include the requirement for an independent external design review following recent learning from other large health sector projects.  Major asset under construction projects have been subject to enhanced reporting arrangement.  Arrangements are in place to ensure that available resource is allocated based on assessed risk criteria and with due regard to deliverability.  The Performance, Assurance, Finance and Infrastructure Committee receive an annual update on progress against backlog maintenance risks. 7.4 Innovation and Transformation In November 2024, the NHS Grampian Chief Executive Team, including IJB Chief Officers, established a Strategic Change Board (SCB) to support the coordination and delivery of strategic change activity across the system. The SCB brings together programmes of work that require cross-system collaboration, shared governance, and the conditions necessary for transformation. This recognises the critical importance of working in collaboration with system partners on shared priorities across the health and care system at a local, regional and national level. Recognising the scale of the challenge, a Route Map for Strategic Change is being developed with partners across NHS Grampian and the three IJBs. It will set out system-wide priorities and transformation ambitions through to 2028/29, aligned to a shared commitment to improve outcomes, shift demand, and embed sustainability and prevention into the way the system operates. A key component of this work is the co-design of a Grampian Model of Change — a consistent and practical approach to how change is led, delivered and embedded across the system. The Route Map will be considered by the NHS Grampian Board in summer 2025. It is intended to support coordinated, long-term and sustainable action across the health and care system, grounded in partnership and focused on delivering what matters to people. The Board launched a revised digital strategy ‘Service Transformation through Digital: a strategy 2023 – 2027” in December 2023 and this has progressed during the year. The board undertook the following innovation and transformation measures in the year in support of this strategy:  The implementation of the Morse system across Community Nursing and Community Child Health services in Grampian replacing fragmented paper- based systems and inadequate electronic applications with a unified, modern Electronic Patient Record.  Working closely with the Centre for Sustainable Delivery and the Scottish Health Technology Group, we delivered an evaluation of using an AI product to transform the lung cancer pathway. The product was used to risk stratify chest x-rays and enabled accelerated early diagnostics and reporting. This resulted in a reduction in the time it takes to get 95% of people to treatment by 30 days, and identification of 12% more treatable cancers.  We evaluated whether we could replace one of the two first readers in the national breast cancer screening programme, with an AI product, whilst retaining a third reader where there was disagreement. This work involved close collaboration with the industry partner and the University of Aberdeen colleagues. Results highlighted that workload could be reduced by 36% and an additional 10% of cancers were identified. NHS Grampian won the Digital Health & Care Awards 2025 for Data Driven Innovation for this work.  NHS Grampian was the lead board for a £10M Once-for-Scotland programme evaluating the utility of a future drone technology network to support the delivery of routine and urgent health care. This is a complex programme involving almost every health board in Scotland, multiple industry partners and universities. Live flights and simulations focused on a wide range of use cases including the delivery of chemotherapy to district hospitals, and transportation of blood and organs for transfusions and transplants. 7.5 Workforce Workforce costs are in excess of 60% of the revenue spend, in the context of the very significant financial pressures the Board faces, and given the limited levers available to reduce the size and cost of the workforce because of longstanding national policy. Written evidence relating to the performance ratings for Executive members, following review by the Chief Executive and Chair, is made available to the Remuneration Committee for approval. For the Chief Executive, the process is undertaken by the Chair.

B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT For Non-Executive members, the process is also undertaken by the Chair and written evidence is submitted to the Cabinet Secretary for Health and Wellbeing. The progression of the Executive and Senior Management Cohort through the pay range is subject to, as a minimum, the fully acceptable performance of the individual. A National Performance Management Committee as detailed in PCS (ESM) 2019/1 ensures, on behalf of the Scottish Government, the effective and consistent application of pay and performance management arrangements for NHS Scotland Executives subject to Ministerial Direction. Remuneration Committee The Remuneration Committee ensures that arrangements are in place to comply with NHS Grampian policy and Scottish Government direction and guidance for determining the employment, remuneration and terms and conditions of employment and for approving the personal objectives of all Executive Directors and Senior Managers. Further information including membership of the Remuneration Committee, is included in the Governance Statement on Pages 55 to 56 above. B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT 2. STAFF REPORT 1. The Workforce Our staff are at the centre of the organisation, their commitment is pivotal to all the services that are delivered across the region and we would like to take this opportunity to recognise and thank them. The last year has been one of continual service pressure and demand along with the current financial challenges. We continue to substantively employ over 17,000 staff, however in the last year a vacancy management process has been implemented which has led to a stabilisation of the workforce and a significant drop in turnover with vacancy levels now lower than previous years. Work continues to reduce the level and related costs of supplementary staff. Successful implementation of initiatives to improvement workforce supply was progressed during the year. NHS Grampian continues to maintain a lower sickness absence rate than the majority of boards in Scotland. These rates are reported to Chief Executive Team and Staff Governance Committee (SGC) by profession, portfolio, directorate and overall organisational levels. Analysis is provided on reasons and types of absence and a number of projects are in place to support staff’s health and wellbeing and consequently improve the sickness absence rates. Occupational Health, managers/supervisors and operational HR all contribute to managing staff and supporting them to be in work. Ensuring Good Staff Governance NHS Grampian works in Partnership with staff side colleagues to achieve the NHS Scotland Staff Governance Standard. This places requirements on the organisation to ensure that all staff are: 1. Well-informed; 2. appropriately trained and developed; 3. involved in decisions; 4. treated fairly and consistently, with dignity and respect, in an environment where diversity is valued; and 5. provided with a continuously improving and safe working environment, promoting the health and wellbeing of staff, patients and the wider community. Through the Staff Governance Committee, a cycle of reporting is in place relating to agreed objectives linked to the Annual Delivery Plan and Plan for the Future. Through the Grampian Area Partnership Forum and local Partnership Fora, BMA (British Medical Association) Joint Negotiating Committee and the Area Clinical Forum, staff and trade union representatives and managers meet to discuss and consult on pertinent issues.

B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT We continue to use our NHS Grampian extant policies as well as working with the growing number of Once for Scotland Policies and their associated guidance, ensuring any new polices are shared for consultation. Culture and Wellbeing The Board’s Plan for the Future makes a commitment to balance enabling wellness whilst responding to illness, good quality care and building sustainable health services. It articulates an ambition to change the relationship with the public as a key enabler. We continue to ensure our staff our informed and involved in decisions through regular communication such as the daily global brief, the SharePoint Intranet page and the monthly Senior Leadership Team meetings, which engages leaders from across the system, providing information and seeking views and input on key matters. We remain committed to supporting staff to Speak Up when they have concerns. In addition to our 17 active Speak-up Ambassadors (SuA's), we have a 15 hour per week Whistleblowing and Speak Up Manager role, the role involves ensuring the SuA's have the skills, confidence and support required to help signpost colleagues who have concerns. We have also held a whistleblowing 'Pause and Reflect' workshop to learn from how we have been handling whistleblowing concerns. Participants included Staff Governance Committee members and other staff with roles involved in whistleblowing, which resulted in an action plan being produced with improvement ideas and opportunities. During the year a report providing assurance as to the effective implementation of the Whistleblowing Standards, introduced in April 2021 was considered by the Board. Staff Development Throughout 2024/25 there was a focus to support staff to increase their completion rates for statutory and mandatory training. To support staff development we offer a range of programmes and initiatives, some examples being: the Leading Empowered Organisations course, now for clinical and non- clinical staff; promote iMatter and the related team action plans; training a cross system group of mediators; providing numeracy training and encouraging staff to be numeracy champions; coaching and mediation services (and training). As an Anchor organisation we run a range of programmes to support employability for current and new staff, including, Project Search, a Nurse Cadet Scheme with Aberdeen Foyer, work placements with Barnardo’s Scotland and foundation, modern and graduate apprenticeships. Specific work looking at internal employability commenced in 2024/25 with Domestic staff. The Health and Care (Staffing) (Scotland) Act 2019 came into effect from 1 st April 2024 has also informed workforce planning. The Act seeks to enable high quality and improved outcomes for people and also supports the health, safety and wellbeing of patients and the wellbeing of staff. The first annual report in relation to NHS Grampian application of the obligation under the act highlights limited Assurance. This report has been developed through engagement with services, individual Portfolio self-assessment, identifying

B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT supportive policies and procedures. The implementation of the Agenda for Change non- pay reforms is likely to impact on our ability to progress to substantial assurance. It has implications for around 12,000 staff and specific duties relating to ensuring that appropriate staffing and processes are in place; if not, to mitigate the risks this creates; seek support/clinical advice; report severe and recurring risks; and ensure time for and to train staff in their related responsibilities. Workforce supply challenges mean that NHS Grampian continues to utilise supplementary staff to provide support safe health care and flexibility across the organisation to meet the workforce gaps and service demands. These gaps include areas where the supply of staff, mainly clinical, is recognised nationally as an enduring challenge. Work continues through to reduce the reliance on Agency Nursing and Medical locums, reduce the number of band 3 rotas for doctors in training, and increase the proportion of locums who are directly engaged. A numbers of initiatives have continued to be progressed to improve workforce supply. The North of Scotland international recruitment service hosted by the Board recruited since January 2024, 24 Adult and 3 Mental Health Nurses. The first Scottish Learning Disability Education Programme has been delivered in Grampian with 3 learning Disability Nurses passing their Objective Structured Clinical Examination Exam first time. For senior medical staff the creation of an alternative recruitment pipeline has been successful in appointing a number of Psychiatry posts, using a General Medical Council approved programme to support international doctors to gain their Certificate in Eligibility for Specialist Registration in a discipline with enduring Consultant vacancies. NHS Grampian has also gained GMC approval for a multi-specialty Specialty and Specialist Fellowship programme which can facilitate similar international recruitment across other specialities, active recruitment pending. As an Anchor organisation we run a range of programmes to support employability for current and new staff, including, Project Search, a Nurse Cadet Scheme with Aberdeen Foyer, work placements with Barnardo’s Scotland and foundation, modern and graduate apprenticeships. Specific work looking at internal employability commenced in 2024/25 with Domestic staff.

Sickness absence NHS Grampian has a dedicated confidential Occupational Health Service (OHS) which provides proactive and reactive supports to keep staff well and in work. This includes the Wellbeing Hub which provides tailored mental health and wellbeing

B REMUNERATION AND STAFF REPORT wellbeing information and services based on their individual circumstances, it includes - counselling (in house funded), Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (funded by NES), and Clinical Psychology (funded by NES). The online manager referral portal enables effective local handling of absence where appropriate and maximise capacity for those most in need of support. Where there are concerns that work could be affecting a member of staff’s health or that a health problem could be affecting an employee’s ability to do their job, specialist Occupational Health advice is available. These inputs assist managers in supporting staff who are absent in the workplace due to sickness. Staff are encouraged to return to work as soon as possible, which may include a 4 week adjustment programme. We continue to provide health surveillance and medicals to staff groups, working with managers and health and safety colleagues to improve the value of risk assessment. NHS Grampian OHS continue to make improvements on learning from adverse events, audit and feedback from users of the service. The annual sickness absence rate for last year was 5.01% (2023/24 4.97%). Long term sickness absence is 2.66% and short term is 2.35% for the same period. The greatest percentage of hours lost against short term absences was Cold, Cough and Flu at 22.1%. For long term absence, Anxiety, Stress, Depression, Other Psychiatric illness remains the top reason with 30% of all hours lost. Work is ongoing to make improvements to absence reporting in relation to the reasons for absence, alongside improvements to attendance management processes and the ongoing wellbeing support. 6. Staff policies applied during the financial year relating to the employment of Disabled Persons As an equal opportunities employer, NHS Grampian welcomes applications for employment from disabled persons and actively seeks to provide an environment where they and any employees who become disabled can continue to contribute to the work of the Board. During 2024/25 NHS Grampian employed an average 78.04 WTE (headcount 96.5) staff with a disability compared to 76.1 WTE (headcount 92) in 2023/24. The NHS Grampian Equality, Diversity and Human Rights Policy can be accessed at the following link https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/. This covers, in detail, the employment of disabled persons including recruitment and selection, treatment during employment and monitoring arrangements. The policy also lists the other NHS Grampian Policies which are in place to ensure that applicants for posts with a disability and staff in post with a disability are treated fairly and that everything reasonably possible is done to meet their needs. This includes providing appropriate support and making reasonable adjustments, training, career development and promotion of disabled persons.
